exemplar.title: How does varying the tilt angle of a solar panel affect its voltage output under a fixed artificial light source?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does varying the tilt angle of a solar panel affect its voltage output under a fixed artificial light source?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

versions.error: Durable Object reset because its code was updated.

strengths: The report uses appropriate discipline-specific terminology in key sections, aiding clarity of technical description.
weaknesses: Structural inconsistencies and merged subsections reduce navigability; figure placement and labeling are uneven.
Verbose colloquialisms and grammatical slips interrupt concision and coherence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With all communication strands at band 2, the report demonstrates basic structure and terminology but frequent organizational and language issues impede clarity. A score of 1 reflects this limited but present communication competence.

strengths: The research question is focused and precisely identifies independent, dependent, and controlled variables, providing a coherent experimental foundation.
Background discussion effectively links solarâtilt optimization to carbonâemission reduction and urban space constraints, demonstrating clear relevance to climateâchange mitigation.
weaknesses: Quantitative context (e.g., specific emissions saved or cost savings) is lacking, reducing depth of environmental relevance.
Connections between optimized tilt and realâworld factors (natural vs. artificial light, local case studies) are not fully explored.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments (5, 5, 4) indicate strong performance across question formulation and context, but minor omissions (lack of quantitative context and fuller linkage to light sources) prevent the top band. A holistic score of 4 reflects solid, coherent framing without exemplary depth.

strengths: The method is clearly described, repeatable, and capable of collecting relevant voltage data across the full angle range.
Materials and equipment listings (Vernier probe, stands, controlled environment) are comprehensive and support reproducibility.
weaknesses: Sampling strategy lacks justification for the chosen number of trials and angle intervals, limiting understanding of statistical reliability.
Risk and ethical considerations are outlined descriptively but omit key safety aspects (e.g., eye protection, electrical ratings) and deeper ethical rationale.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With strand scores of 5 (method design), 3 (sampling), and 3 (risk/ethics), the planning is sound and repeatable but lacks justification and comprehensive safety analysis. A score of 3 captures adequate design with notable omissions.

strengths: The evaluation links the experimental conclusion to sustainability goals and proposes meaningful procedural improvements.
A clear table of strengths and weaknesses demonstrates awareness of method reliability.
weaknesses: Evaluation of conclusions in environmental context is brief and omits socioâeconomic trade-offs or scalability considerations.
Suggested extensions stop short of significant research advances (e.g., different panel types, seasonal modelling).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With all strands at band 3, the work shows descriptive evaluation and sensible improvements but lacks comprehensive critical analysis and transformative extensions. A score of 2 reflects solid but limited evaluation.

strengths: Data are tabulated and presented in line plots that identify the voltageâangle trend, and mean and standard deviation are correctly calculated.
The conclusion that ~30Â° is optimal is directly supported by observed data patterns.
weaknesses: Graphical presentation has labelling errors, missing units, and inconsistent error bars across plots.
Analysis is descriptive without inferential statistics or deeper exploration of variability causes.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strands scored 3 (presentation), 3 (analysis), and 5 (conclusion). While conclusions are well supported, omissions in graph clarity and full statistical analysis limit this section to a holistic 3.

strengths: The proposed application (optimizing tilt to reduce panel number and cost) is explicitly justified based on investigation findings and linked to carbonâreduction targets.
weaknesses: Solution evaluation remains surface-level, lacking feasibility analysis, economic estimations, and unintendedâconsequence discussion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments of 3 (application proposal) and 2 (solution evaluation) yield an average of ~2.5. A final score of 2 captures strong justification but limited critical evaluation of the proposed solution.

strandSelections.reasoning: Appropriate ESS/physics terminology (voltage, standard deviation, renewable) is used, yet wording is verbose with colloquialisms (âsqueeze out,â âmessed withâ).  Hence either concision or terminology is achieved, but not both consistentlyâband 2.

The student links the optimum angle to global sustainability initiatives and recognises the artificial-light limitation, but evaluation of how the conclusion feeds back into the broader environmental issue is brief and omits economic or social trade-offs.  This fits âevaluates â¦ with omissionsâ: 3.

The conclusion clearly states the optimum angle (~30Â°) and is directly supported by the trend in the data and reference to mean values.  Minor caveats about variability are noted, but there is no quantified confidence interval, so the work is strong yet not exemplary; 5 is appropriate.

Mean and standard deviation are calculated correctly and trends are identified.  No inferential statistics (t-test, ANOVA) are attempted and variability is only qualitatively discussed, so analysis is correct but incomplete: hence 3.

Suggested improvements (increase trials, natural sunlight, light meter, digital inclinometer) are meaningful and directly tackle key weaknesses, but they stop short of proposing significant extensions (e.g., testing different panel types or conducting statistical modelling).  Thus the strand earns 3.

The method specifies angles, distance (1 m), trial number, equipment (Vernier probe) and an indoor dark room to control extraneous light, so it is repeatable and evidently capable of providing relevant voltage data across the full range of interest.  Weaknesses (no light-meter verification, hand-built cardboard stands) prevent a flawless design, so a 5 is justified rather than 6.

Links between maximising panel efficiency, needing fewer panels and economic/environmental benefits are described and generally convincing, but the explanation is not fully comprehensive; for instance, no quantitative estimate of emissions saved is attempted and the influence of artificial versus natural light is not explored in the context statement.  The strand therefore meets the âdescribes â¦ with some omissionsâ level (upper end), warranting 4.

The student frames a single, specific question that clearly identifies both the independent variable (tilt angle) and the dependent variable (voltage output) as well as the controlled light source.  The wording is precise and unambiguous, so the question is coherent and focused.  Minor wording lapses (âvarying the tilt â¦ under a fixed artificial light sourceâ) do not reduce clarity, but there is no further sub-questioning or explicit hypothesis structure that might push the quality to the very top of the band; therefore 5 rather than 6 is most appropriate.

The write-up notes strengths (cost-effective, space-saving) and weaknesses (location dependence, weather impacts) of the proposed solution, but the evaluation is surface-level and does not consider feasibility analyses or unintended consequences.  Therefore 2 is appropriate.

Headings follow IB sections and the report is largely ordered, but some subsections merge (evaluation blended with conclusion) and figure placement is inconsistent.  Thus structure exists but is not sustained flawlesslyâscore 2.

Strengths (controlled environment) and weaknesses (angle precision, light intensity) are tabulated, with some discussion of reliability.  Limitations such as spectral mismatch or panel temperature are not addressed in depth.  The work is descriptive rather than fully discursive, warranting 3.

The write-up discusses the global need for renewable energy, the role of solar power in cutting carbon emissions, space constraints in urban areas and the potential 1â2 % gain from optimal tilt.  This goes beyond a simple outline and shows clear relevance to climate-change mitigation, fulfilling the âdiscussesâ descriptor.  Some parts are brief and rely on secondary sources without deeper analysis of local scale impacts, so the strand sits solidly at 5, not 6.

The student explains five repeats âfor dependable dataâ and to calculate mean and standard deviation, which is a description of the strategy.  However, there is little justification of why five trials were chosen, why those particular angles were selected or how sample size affects statistical confidence, so the work does not reach the justification level.  Hence 3.

Key hazards (electric shock, hot glue burns, tripping, lamp overheating) are outlined and brief mitigations provided; ethical notes address reuse of cardboard.  The coverage is adequate but not comprehensiveâno mention of eye safety from bright LEDs or electrical current ratingsâso the strand sits in the 3â4 band; 3 reflects the descriptive rather than detailed nature.

Tables list all raw and processed data, and two graphs are included (line plot of averages plus combined trial plot).  Axes are labelled but one graph has unclear units and error bars only for one angle.  Therefore presentation is appropriate but with omissions, matching the 3â4 band at 3.

The applicationâoptimising tilt to reduce panel number and costâis explicitly justified using the investigationâs findings and linked to carbon-reduction goals.  The narrative shows clear cause-and-effect reasoning, so full credit of 3 is merited.

Logical progression is generally clear, but frequent grammatical slips and repetition occasionally impede flow (e.g., duplicated phrases, misplaced capitals).  The work remains understandable, placing it at âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ: 2.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly focused, identifying independent, dependent, and controlled variables precisely. This coherence strengthens the investigationâs foundation.
Consider refining the question to include the expected trend (e.g., specifying a hypothesized optimal angle range) to frame a testable prediction explicitly.
The background effectively links solar tilt optimization to carbon-emission reduction and urban space constraints, demonstrating relevance to climate change mitigation.
The discussion lacks quantitative context on potential emissions saved or cost savings. Including local data or case studies would deepen the environmental relevance.
Connections between optimized tilt and economic benefits are outlined, but the role of artificial versus natural light in real-world settings is not fully explored.
The prediction states a trend but is vague about why 30â40Â° is optimal (âgreen matchâ unclear). Clarify the theoretical basis for the hypothesis.
Variables are clearly listed, but the description of uncontrolled factors mentions only temperature. Consider listing other uncontrolled variables (humidity, spectral changes).
The materials list is comprehensive, detailing measurement and data-collection instruments, which supports reproducibility.
Light intensity is assumed constant; include calibration using a light meter or lux sensor to confirm consistency across all trials.
The setup photographs lack scale or labeling; annotating images would improve clarity and support reproducibility.
Procedure lists steps clearly, but repeating steps 2 and 7 duplicates information. Consolidate to avoid redundancy.
Justification for five trials lacks reference to statistical confidence. Discuss how sample size affects reliability and error margins.
Electrical safety and hot-glue risks are addressed, but no mention of eye protection under bright LED lamps. Include PPE considerations.
The ethical consideration of recycling cardboard is commendable, demonstrating environmental responsibility.
Table 1 presents raw and processed data, but column headings (e.g., âtrailâ typo) and units in axes need correction for clarity.
Error bars appear only in Figure 9; include them in all graphs to visually represent variability across all angles.
Mean and standard deviation calculations are correct, but no inferential statistics are applied. Consider simple t-tests to assess significance between angles.
The analysis correctly identifies the 30Â° peak, but lacks discussion of why variability is higher at that angle (e.g., protractor precision).
The conclusion is supported by data trends and notes variability; it succinctly states the optimum angle.
Evaluation of conclusions in environmental context is brief; include economic or social trade-offs of implementing optimal tilts at scale.
Method evaluation notes stability and reliability but omits discussion of panel temperature effects or spectral mismatch; these limitations should be addressed.
Improvement suggestions are meaningful (more trials, natural sunlight), but consider significant extensions (e.g., tracking systems, seasonal variations).
The improvement table effectively links limitations to actionable steps, demonstrating solid evaluation of procedure.
The table layout is dense and could be split by category or simplifiedâlong text cells reduce readability. Consider bullet lists for clarity.
Application discussion effectively connects findings to real-world energy policies and urban constraints, providing clear relevance.
Solution evaluation notes strengths and weaknesses but remains surface-level; include feasibility analysis, cost estimates, and potential unintended consequences.
The report uses appropriate discipline-specific terminology, but occasional colloquialisms and verbosity reduce concision.
Logical flow is generally clear, though some sections (e.g., merging of evaluation and conclusion) impede seamless progression.
Several headings (e.g., duplicated âB. Analysisâ labels) overlap or are misplaced, disrupting navigability. Align section naming consistently.
One citation link appears truncated or malformed. Ensure consistency and completeness in reference formatting.
